name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
تَبَارَكَ ٱلَّذِى نَزَّلَ ٱلْفُرْقَانَ عَلَىٰ عَبْدِهِۦ لِيَكُونَ لِلْعَـٰلَمِينَ نَذِيرًا (١)
```

```english
Blessed is He who sent down the Criterion to His servant that he may be a warner to all the nations. (1)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِى لَهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَمْ يَتَّخِذْ وَلَدًا وَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُۥ شَرِيكٌ فِى ٱلْمُلْكِ وَخَلَقَ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ فَقَدَّرَهُۥ تَقْدِيرًا (٢)
```

```english
He, to whom belongs the sovereignty of the heavens and the earth, and who did not take any offspring, nor has He any partner in sovereignty, and He created everything and determined it in a precise measure. (2)
```

```arabic
وَٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ ءَالِهَةً لَّا يَخْلُقُونَ شَيْـًٔا وَهُمْ يُخْلَقُونَ وَلَا يَمْلِكُونَ لِأَنفُسِهِمْ ضَرًّا وَلَا نَفْعًا وَلَا يَمْلِكُونَ مَوْتًا وَلَا حَيَوٰةً وَلَا نُشُورًا (٣)
```

```english
Yet they have taken gods besides Him who create nothing and have themselves been created, and who have no control over their own harm or benefit and have no control over [their own] death, life, or resurrection. (3)
```

## (Commentary)

The purpose of the Surah is to clarify that the call of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is a truthful call arising from a divine message on behalf of Allah, the Exalted, and a Book revealed from Him. It pays profound attention to refuting the objections raised by the disbelievers (الكفار) regarding the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) being a messenger from Allah and his Book being revealed from Him, returning to this theme time and again.

This objective entails some argumentation for monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — and the negation of any partner (الشريك), as well as mentioning some descriptions of the Day of Resurrection and a brief account of the beautiful attributes of the believers (المؤمنين). The discourse therein follows the style of warning (الإنذار) and instilling fear rather than glad tidings.

The Surah is Meccan, as evidenced by the context of the vast majority of its verses. However, three verses are sometimes excepted, namely His saying, the Exalted: **وَالَّذِينَ لا يَدْعُونَ مَعَ اللهِ إِلهاً آخَرَ** — "Those who do not invoke another deity besides Allah..." until His saying: **غَفُوراً رَحِيماً** — "...All-forgiving, All-merciful." Perhaps the reason for this exception is that they contain the legislation of the prohibition of adultery (الزنا). However, you have already learned from what we cited regarding the reports on the verse of wine in Surah al-Ma'idah that adultery and wine were known to be prohibited in Islam from the very beginning of the Islamic call.

Strangely, some have claimed that the entire Surah is Medinan except for the first three verses: **تَبارَكَ الَّذِي** — "Blessed is He..." until His saying: **نُشُوراً** — "...resurrection."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **تَبارَكَ الَّذِي نَزَّلَ الْفُرْقانَ عَلى عَبْدِهِ لِيَكُونَ لِلْعالَمِينَ نَذِيراً** — **Blessed is He who sent down the Criterion to His servant that he may be a warner to all the worlds.** (1)

Blessing (البركة) refers to the permanence of good in a thing, similar to the settling of water in a pool (البركة). It is derived from the verb "the camel knelt" (برك البعير) when it places its chest upon the ground and settles there. From this comes *tabaraka* (تبارك) in the sense of the permanence of abundant good. The form of the word indicates intensification (المبالغة), as has been said. It is almost exclusively used for Allah, the Exalted, and is not applied to others except rarely.

The Criterion (الفرقان) — *the decisive standard that distinguishes truth from falsehood* — is the "distinction" (الفرق). The Quran is named thus because its verses were revealed separately (متفرقة), or because it distinguishes truth from falsehood. This latter meaning is supported by the application of the term "Criterion" (الفرقان) in His speech, the Exalted, to the Torah as well, despite it being revealed all at once. Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "The Criterion (الفرقان) is more emphatic than 'distinction' (الفرق) because it is used for the distinction between truth and falsehood... it is a noun, not a verbal noun (مصدر) according to what has been said, whereas 'distinction' (الفرق) is used for that and for other things." End quote.

"All the worlds" (العالمون) is the plural of "world" (عالم), meaning the creation (الخلق). It is said in *al-Sihah*: "The world (العالم) is the creation, and the plural is *al-awalim* (العوالم), and *al-alamun* (العالمون) refers to the various types of creation." End quote. Although the term encompasses all creation—inanimate objects, plants, animals, humans, jinn, and angels—the context of the verse, which makes warning (الإنذار) the goal of the Quran's revelation, indicates that the intended meaning is the legally accountable beings (المكلفين) among the creation. These are the two weighty groups (الثقلان): humans and jinn, as far as we know.

From this, it becomes clear that the claim made by some—that the verse indicates the universality of his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) message to everything other than Allah—is untenable. Such a view overlooks the reason for expressing the message in terms of "warning" (الإنذار). Similar to this verse is His saying, the Exalted: **وَاصْطَفاكِ عَلى نِساءِ الْعالَمِينَ** — "...and chosen you above the women of all the worlds" (3:42), and His saying: **وَفَضَّلْناهُمْ عَلَى الْعالَمِينَ** — "...and preferred them over all the worlds" (45:16).

The "warner" (النذير) means the one who warns (المنذر), and "warning" (الإنذار) is close in meaning to "frightening."

Thus, His saying, the Exalted: **تَبارَكَ الَّذِي نَزَّلَ الْفُرْقانَ عَلى عَبْدِهِ** — "Blessed is He who sent down the Criterion to His servant" means that abundant good has been established and realized in Him who sent down the Criterion upon His servant Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). The establishment of abundant good returning to the creation within Him, the Exalted, is an allusion (كناية) to its overflowing from Him upon His creation, as He revealed to His servant a Book that distinguishes between truth and falsehood, saving all the worlds from misguidance and driving them toward guidance (الهدى) — *the divine leading toward truth and the straight path*.

The combination in the verse between the revelation of the Quran from Him, the Exalted, and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) being a messenger from Him and a warner to all the worlds—along with naming the Quran a "Criterion" (فرقان) between truth and falsehood and describing the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) as being His "servant" (عبدا) and a "warner" (نذيرا), which implies his being owned and commanded, possessing nothing of himself—all of this is a preparation for what He will later narrate regarding the polytheists (المشركين). This includes their disparagement of the Quran as a fabrication against Allah that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) invented with the help of others, and their disparagement of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) because he eats food and walks in the markets, and other things they uttered—and that which refutes their criticisms.

The summary is that it is a Book that distinguishes, by its brilliant proof, between truth and falsehood; thus, it can only be truth, for falsehood does not distinguish between truth and falsehood, but rather falsehood is made to resemble truth to deceive people. Furthermore, the one who brought it is a servant obedient to Allah, warning all the worlds with it and calling them to the truth; thus, he can only be upon the truth. Had he been a follower of falsehood, he would not have called to the truth but would have deviated and swerved from it. Moreover, Allah, the Glorified, testifies in His miraculous speech to the truthfulness of his message and that the Book he brought is revealed from Him.

From here, the weakness of the claim made by some becomes apparent: that "the Criterion" (الفرقان) refers to all the heavenly books revealed to the prophets, and "His servant" refers to all the prophets (عليه السلام). Its distance from the apparent meaning of the wording is not hidden.

His saying, the Exalted: **لِيَكُونَ لِلْعالَمِينَ نَذِيراً** — "that he may be a warner to all the worlds." The letter *lam* is for causality (التعليل), indicating that the purpose of sending down the Criterion upon His servant is for him to be a warner to all the worlds of humans and jinn. The plural form defined by the article *al-* (الجمع المحلى باللام) signifies exhaustiveness (الاستغراق). The use of this plural form is not without an indication that all have one God, unlike the view of the idolaters (الوثنيون) where each people takes a god other than what others take.

The mention of warning (الإنذار) alone, without glad tidings, is because the discourse in the Surah is directed toward warning and instilling fear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **الَّذِي لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** — **to whom belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth...** (2) until the end of the verse. Ownership (الملك) — with a *kasrah* or *fathah* on the *mim* — is the subsistence of a thing by another such that the latter may dispose of it as he wishes. This is true whether it is the subsistence of its essence (رقبته), like the subsistence of the essence of property by its owner such that he has various types of disposal over it, or its subsistence by his dominance over it through disposal by command, prohibition, and various types of governance, like a king's dominance over his subjects and what is in their hands. The second type is often called *mulk* (الملك) with a *dammah* on the *mim*.

Thus, *milk* (الملك) — with a *kasrah* — is more general than *mulk* (الملك) — with a *dammah*. As al-Raghib said: "The *malik* (الملك) — with a *fathah* on the *mim* and *kasrah* on the *lam* — is the one who disposes by command and prohibition among the masses, and this is specific to the governance of rational beings; hence it is said 'king of the people' and not 'king of things'..." until he said: "So *mulk* (الملك) — with a *dammah* — is the control of the thing disposed of through governance, and *milk* (الملك) — with a *kasrah* — is like the genus for *mulk*. Every *mulk* (الملك) — with a *dammah* — is *milk* (الملك) — with a *kasrah* — but not every *milk* is *mulk*." End quote.

Sometimes *milk* (الملك) — with a *kasrah* — is restricted to what pertains to the essence (الرقبة), and *mulk* (الملك) — with a *dammah* — to other than that.

So His saying, the Exalted: **الَّذِي لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** — "to whom belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth" — where the *lam* denotes exclusive possession (الاختصاص) — implies that the heavens and the earth are owned by Him, not independent in themselves in any of their aspects, nor self-sufficient from His disposal through governance. Governance within them and the turning of their gears is exclusive to Allah, the Exalted; He is the Sovereign (المليك) who disposes through governance over them absolutely.

By this, the connection of His saying: **وَلَمْ يَتَّخِذْ وَلَداً** — "...and He has not taken a son" to what preceded it becomes clear. Absolute sovereignty leaves no need for taking a son, for taking a son is for one of two reasons: either because the person is not strong enough to manage all his affairs and does not possess the management of them all, so he takes a son to assist him in some of his needs—and Allah, the Glorified, owns everything and is powerful over whatever He wills; or because the person is limited in duration, owning what he owns only for a limited term, so he takes a son to succeed him and manage his affairs after him—and Allah, the Glorified, owns everything eternally (سرمدا) and is not subject to extinction or cessation. Thus, He has no need whatsoever to take a son. In this is a refutation of the polytheists and the Christians.

Likewise, His saying after it: **وَلَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُ شَرِيكٌ فِي الْمُلْكِ** — "...nor has He any partner in the kingdom." The need for a partner only arises when the sovereignty does not encompass all matters. But His ownership, the Exalted, is general to all things, encompassing all their aspects, with nothing excluded from it. In this is a refutation of the polytheists.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَخَلَقَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ فَقَدَّرَهُ تَقْدِيراً** — "...and He created everything and ordained it in a precise measure" is a clarification that the management (تدبير) of all matters returns to Him, the Exalted, alone through creation (الخلق) and ordainment (التقدير). He is the Lord of all the worlds (رب العالمين); there is no Lord besides Him.

The explanation of this is that since creation occurs through the mediation of causes (الأسباب) that precede the thing and accompany it, this necessitates the interconnection of the existences of things with one another. Thus, the existence of every thing and the effects of its existence are measured (يتقدر) according to what is ordained by the causes and factors preceding and accompanying it. The events occurring in the world according to the witnessed order are intertwined with creation, following the preceding and accompanying causes and factors. Since there is no creator other than Allah, the Glorified, there is no manager of affairs (مدبر للأمر) other than Him; thus, there is no Lord (رب) who owns things and manages their affairs other than Him.

The fact that He, the Exalted, possesses the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, ruling and disposing in them absolutely, necessitates that creation (الخلقة) subsists through Him; for if it subsisted through another, the kingdom would belong to that other. The subsistence of creation through Him necessitates the subsistence of ordainment (التقدير) through Him, because ordainment is a branch of creation. The subsistence of ordainment through Him necessitates the subsistence of management (التدبير) through Him. Thus, to Him belongs the kingdom and the management; He is the Lord (الرب), exalted is His state.

Although His ownership, the Exalted, of the heavens and the earth necessitates the attribution of creation and ordainment to Him, the idolaters—while admitting the generality of His ownership—believed that His ownership of all and His Lordship over the whole did not negate the ownership and Lordship of their deities over some parts, by His delegating (تفويض) that to them. Thus, each of the deities was a sovereign in the region of its divinity, a Lord for its subjects, while Allah, the Glorified, was the King of kings, the Lord of lords, and the God of gods.

Therefore, His saying: **الَّذِي لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** — "to whom belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth" was not sufficient to prove the exclusivity of Lordship (الربوبية) to Him, the Exalted, against them. Rather, it was necessary to bring His saying: **وَخَلَقَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ فَقَدَّرَهُ تَقْدِيراً** — "...and He created everything and ordained it in a precise measure."

It is as if someone were to say: "Granted that His ownership of the heavens and the earth makes Him independent of taking a son or a partner—which would imply the removal of His ownership from some things—but why is it not possible for Him to take some of His creation as a partner for Himself by delegating some affairs of the world to them, while He remains the owner of them and what He delegated to them?" This is what the polytheists believed; they used to say in the *talbiyah* of the Hajj: "At Your service, You have no partner, except a partner who is Yours; You own him and what he owns."

So it was answered that creation belongs to Him, the Glorified, and ordainment is inseparable from it. When these two combine, management (التدبير) necessarily follows them. Thus, to Him, the Glorified, belongs the management of every thing. There is no ownership alongside His ownership, nor any Lordship alongside His Lordship.

In summary, His saying: **الَّذِي لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَلَمْ يَتَّخِذْ وَلَداً وَلَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُ شَرِيكٌ فِي الْمُلْكِ** — "to whom belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, and He has not taken a son, nor has He any partner in the kingdom" is directed toward the monotheism of Lordship (توحيد الربوبية) and the negation of a son and a partner by way of proving absolute ownership. And His saying: **وَخَلَقَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ فَقَدَّرَهُ تَقْدِيراً** — "...and He created everything and ordained it in a precise measure" is an affirmation and clarification of the meaning of the generality of ownership, and that it is an ownership based upon creation and ordainment, necessitating His, the Exalted, undertaking of every judgment and management without delegating any part of the matter to anyone among the creation.

Regarding this verse and the one before it, they have other opinions which we have overlooked mentioning due to their lack of benefit.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَاتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِهِ آلِهَةً لا يَخْلُقُونَ شَيْئاً وَهُمْ يُخْلَقُونَ** — **They have taken besides Him gods who create nothing and are themselves created...** (3) etc. When He described Himself as the Creator of everything and its Ordainer, and that to Him belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth—and this is how the God who is worshipped must be—He pointed to the misguidance of the polytheists who worshipped idols that do not create anything, but are themselves created and fashioned by them, possessing nothing for themselves or for others.

The pronoun in "they have taken" (وَاتَّخَذُوا) refers to the polytheists as indicated by the context, even if they were not mentioned previously. Such an expression conveys contempt and disdain.

His saying: **مِنْ دُونِهِ آلِهَةً لا يَخْلُقُونَ شَيْئاً وَهُمْ يُخْلَقُونَ** — "...besides Him gods who create nothing and are themselves created" refers to their idols which they fashioned with their own hands through carving or the like. Describing them as "gods" (آلهة) while following it with "who create nothing and are themselves created" is an indication that they have nothing of divinity except a name they have called them by, without any of its reality being realized, as He, the Exalted, said: **إِنْ هِيَ إِلَّا أَسْماءٌ سَمَّيْتُمُوها أَنْتُمْ وَآباؤُكُمْ** — "They are but names which you have named—you and your fathers..." (53:23).

The use of the indefinite noun in His saying: **لا يَخْلُقُونَ شَيْئاً** — "...create nothing" in the context of negation is for emphasis in rebuking them, as they turned away from Allah, the Glorified—who is the Creator of everything—and attached themselves to idols that do not create anything at all. Rather, they are in a worse state than that, as they are fashioned by their worshippers and created by their imaginations. A similar discourse applies to His saying: **ضَرًّا وَلا نَفْعاً** — "...harm or benefit" and His saying: **مَوْتاً وَلا حَياةً وَلا نُشُوراً** — "...death or life or resurrection."

His saying: **وَلا يَمْلِكُونَ لِأَنْفُسِهِمْ ضَرًّا وَلا نَفْعاً** — "...and who have no control over any harm or benefit for themselves" is a negation of ownership (الملك) from them, which is essential for a God. Their worshippers only worship them to ward off harm from themselves and bring benefit to themselves. Since they do not own harm or benefit even for themselves, their worship is nothing but madness and misguidance.

From this, it becomes clear that the inclusion of "for themselves" (لِأَنْفُسِهِمْ) in the context adds to the rebuke. The speech is in the sense of progression (الترقي); that is, they do not own harm for themselves such that they could ward it off, nor benefit such that they could bring it—so how could they for others? Harm has been mentioned before benefit because warding off harm is more important than bringing benefit.

His saying: **وَلا يَمْلِكُونَ مَوْتاً وَلا حَياةً وَلا نُشُوراً** — "...nor have they any control over death or life or resurrection" means they do not own death such that they could ward it off from their worshippers or whomever they wish, nor life such that they could take it from whomever they wish or bestow it upon whomever they wish, nor resurrection (نشورا) such that they could raise people to recompense them for their deeds. The ownership of these matters is among the requirements of divinity.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from Ibn Sinan, from someone he mentioned, who said: I asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the Quran and the Criterion (الفرقان)—are they two things or one thing? He said: "The Quran is the entirety of the Book, and the Criterion (الفرقان) is the clear (المحكم) part whose practice is obligatory."

In *al-Ikhtisas* by al-Mufid: In the tradition of Abdullah ibn Salam to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), he said: "Inform me, did Allah reveal a Book to you?" He said: "Yes." He asked: "And which Book is it?" He said: "The Criterion (الفرقان)." He asked: "And why did your Lord name it the Criterion?" He said: "Because its verses and chapters are separate (متفرق); it was revealed in other than tablets—unlike the other scrolls, the Torah, the Gospel, and the Psalms, which were all revealed in their entirety in tablets and papers." He said: "You have spoken the truth, O Muhammad."

**I say:** Each of the two narrations looks at one of the two previously mentioned meanings of the Criterion (الفرقان).
